picks.headline: 60 acres, class-2 soil, stocked ponds, fenced pasture
picks.reason: The best overall package in the set: USDA soil class 2 (Leadvale silt loam, 1.8% slope) supports genuine row-crop and garden production, not just grazing. Stocked ponds and 25 acres of fenced pasture mean water and livestock infrastructure are already present. At $4,683/acre for 60 acres in Conway County with flood zone X designation, this is priced at a significant discount to its soil quality. Critical unknowns are road surface condition and utility hook-up status — both must be confirmed before closing.

picks.headline: 283 acres with cabin, lowest price per acre
picks.reason: At $1,497/acre, this is the cheapest land per acre in the entire candidate set by a wide margin, and 283.8 acres gives genuine scale for self-sufficiency — timber harvest, grazing rotation, and food production without crowding. Macks Creek provides year-round water, and an existing cabin means immediate shelter. Soil class 3 (Poynor very gravelly silt loam, 6% slope) supports mixed farming with moderate management. The main risks are missing utility and road-access details, which are non-trivial at $425k total — verify both before proceeding.

picks.headline: 80 Ozarks acres with well, electric, and pond
picks.reason: Eighty acres with on-site electric, a functioning well, and a pond for $2,498/acre is strong value — this is the only large-acreage property under $200k with confirmed utilities. Proximity to Norfork Lake adds hunting and foraging range. Soil and flood data are absent, which is a real gap; the property earns the highest homestead fit score in the set (80) based on infrastructure, but buildability and zoning must be independently verified. No HOA and a stated build-ready tag make this a high-priority site visit.

picks.headline: 160 mature-hardwood acres at $2,400 per acre
picks.reason: The second-lowest price per acre in the set ($2,400) for 160 acres of mature hardwood in Wayne County, Missouri, with county road access and class 3 soil (Tilk-Secesh complex, 1% slope, occasionally flooded). Scale and timber alone justify serious consideration — 160 acres supports full-scale wood fuel, building material, and hunting self-sufficiency. The primary and significant caveat is no water source mentioned; well drilling will be required, and the 'occasionally flooded' soil description needs ground-truthing along any creek bottom. Clearwater Lake proximity helps but is not a substitute for on-parcel water.

picks.headline: 80 acres with home, pasture, and timber in Randolph County
picks.reason: An existing home plus 80 acres of mixed pasture and timber at $4,312/acre in flood zone X gives a solid move-in foundation. Soil class 3 (Doniphan gravelly silt loam, 5% slope) supports grazing and gardens. The wet-weather creek is seasonal only — reliable water requires well drilling, which is the primary infrastructure cost to budget before closing. At 550 feet elevation in Randolph County, the growing season is reasonable for the Ozarks.

picks.headline: 103 acres of maintained cattle pasture with ponds
picks.reason: Sixty acres of actively maintained cattle pasture plus ponds on 103 total acres in Wayne County, Missouri at $3,397/acre is a legitimate agricultural buy. Soil class 3 (Tilk-Secesh, 1% slope, occasionally flooded) confirms grazing viability. The creek is seasonal, but ponds provide a buffer for livestock water. The main trade-off is zero existing structures — well, septic, and a dwelling must be built from scratch, which is a substantial capital outlay on top of the $349,900 purchase price.

picks.headline: 37 acres, class-2 soil, Little Red River frontage
picks.reason: Little Red River frontage delivers the most reliable year-round water in this set — a river, not just a pond or well. Soil class 2 (Dela loamy fine sand, 1% slope) is the highest agricultural quality available among these candidates and supports diverse crop production at low elevation (253 feet). Flood zone X status despite river proximity is reassuring. The trade-off is price: at $11,864/acre for 37 acres, this is expensive relative to peers, and proximity to the river warrants a FEMA FIRM panel review and seasonal high-water mark verification before purchase.

picks.headline: 67.5 acres with spring, creek, existing well and septic
picks.reason: Rebel Hollow eliminates two of the largest infrastructure barriers for a raw land buy: an existing well and septic system are in place alongside a year-round spring and creek in Texas County, Missouri at $4,133/acre for 67.5 acres. Multiple independent water sources reduce single-point-of-failure risk for off-grid living. Soil class 4 (Bendavis-Poynor, 5% slope) means food production will be limited to gardens and grazing rather than row crops. Road access quality and electricity status are unconfirmed in the truncated listing — both must be checked.

picks.headline: Off-grid cabin on 37 acres adjacent to Current River
picks.reason: An existing off-grid cabin on 37 acres with Current River proximity at $5,132/acre in Carter County, Missouri offers immediate habitability for remote self-sufficient living. Soil class 3 (Tilk-Secesh, 1% slope) is good quality for the Ozarks. The Current River is a permanent, high-quality water source. Primary risks are the condition of the off-grid systems (must be inspected), legal water rights confirmation, and the absence of flood zone data despite river adjacency — order a flood determination before closing.

picks.headline: 60 Searcy County acres with pond, pasture, and timber
picks.reason: Sixty acres at $3,750/acre with an established pond, open pasture, and hardwood timber in Searcy County, Arkansas addresses the core homesteading needs of water, grazing, and fuel/building material. Soil class 4 (Noark very gravelly silt loam, 5% slope) limits row-crop ambition but is workable for livestock. No flood zone data and no confirmed utility infrastructure are the main gaps; well and septic costs should be budgeted as part of the acquisition plan. A companion parcel (001 Tomahawk Slab Road, ranked just outside the top 12) shares the same watershed.

picks.headline: 40 Ozark acres with cabins and year-round waterfall creek
picks.reason: Ozark Waterfall Retreat offers the most immediately habitable and water-secure setup in Stone County: existing cabins, modern utilities, and multiple year-round water sources (creeks, waterfall) at 1,306-foot elevation. Soil class 3 (Linker Mountainburg complex, 5% slope) supports small-scale agriculture. The primary trade-off is cost — $9,912/acre for 40 acres is near the top of this candidate pool — and the existing grid-tied infrastructure makes transitioning to full off-grid operation more complex. Best suited for comfort-level rural living rather than pioneering off-grid setup.

picks.headline: 27 acres with slab, septic, electric, water already in
picks.reason: The only property in this set with a concrete slab, existing septic, electric, and water already permitted and installed on 27 acres in Wayne County, Missouri at $6,851/acre. Infrastructure costs that typically run $30,000–$80,000 are already sunk, which partially offsets the higher per-acre price. Soil class 3 (Cornwall silt loam, 6% slope) supports mixed farming. At 27 acres it is the smallest large-acreage pick here, limiting long-term expansion for livestock or large gardens — ranked last because scale constraints are a real homesteading ceiling.
